content.text: The Problem Sentence-Window Retrieval Solves

Sentence-level embeddings are extremely precise — a single sentence encodes one focused idea and matches narrow queries with high accuracy. But a single sentence almost never provides enough context for an LLM to generate a complete, grounded answer. Sentence-window retrieval separates the two concerns: each sentence is indexed as its own embedding unit for retrieval precision, but when a sentence is matched, the system returns the surrounding window of sentences (the matched sentence plus k neighbours on each side) to the LLM. The retrieval granularity is fine; the generation context is rich.

attrs.alt: Sentence-window retrieval: S3 matches the query, the window S1-S5 is returned to the LLM
attrs.title: Sentence-Window Retrieval

content.text: Indexing with LlamaIndex SentenceWindowNodeParser

LlamaIndex provides SentenceWindowNodeParser which splits a document into sentence nodes and attaches the surrounding window as metadata on each node. At query time, the MetadataReplacementPostprocessor replaces the single-sentence node text with the full window text before the nodes are passed to the LLM synthesiser.

# pip install llama-index llama-index-embeddings-openai
from llama_index.core import SimpleDirectoryReader, VectorStoreIndex, Settings
from llama_index.core.node_parser import SentenceWindowNodeParser
from llama_index.core.postprocessor import MetadataReplacementPostProcessor
from llama_index.embeddings.openai import OpenAIEmbedding

Settings.embed_model = OpenAIEmbedding()

# 1. Parse: each node = one sentence, metadata["window"] = surrounding context
node_parser = SentenceWindowNodeParser.from_defaults(
    window_size=3,                     # 3 sentences on each side
    window_metadata_key="window",      # key where window text is stored
    original_text_key="original_text", # key where the raw sentence is stored
)

documents = SimpleDirectoryReader("./docs").load_data()
nodes = node_parser.get_nodes_from_documents(documents)

# 2. Index sentence nodes (small, precise embeddings)
index = VectorStoreIndex(nodes)

# 3. At query time, replace each retrieved sentence with its window
postprocessor = MetadataReplacementPostProcessor(target_metadata_key="window")

query_engine = index.as_query_engine(
    similarity_top_k=2,               # retrieve 2 most similar sentences
    node_postprocessors=[postprocessor],  # replace them with their windows
)

response = query_engine.query("how does self-attention calculate weights?")
print(response)
Manual Implementation with LangChain

In LangChain there is no built-in sentence-window retriever, but the pattern is straightforward to implement manually. Split the document into sentences with NLTK or spaCy, build sentence nodes with window metadata, embed the single sentences, and at retrieval time substitute the window text before passing to the LLM.

import nltk
from langchain_core.documents import Document
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings

nltk.download("punkt", quiet=True)

def build_sentence_window_docs(
    text: str,
    source: str,
    window_size: int = 3,
) -> list[Document]:
    """Split text into sentence nodes with window context stored in metadata."""
    sentences = nltk.sent_tokenize(text)
    docs = []
    for i, sentence in enumerate(sentences):
        start = max(0, i - window_size)
        end   = min(len(sentences), i + window_size + 1)
        window = " ".join(sentences[start:end])
        docs.append(Document(
            page_content=sentence,  # embedded: single sentence
            metadata={
                "window": window,   # returned to LLM: full window
                "sentence_idx": i,
                "source": source,
            }
        ))
    return docs

# Index
embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings()
sentence_docs = build_sentence_window_docs(long_text, source="doc1.txt")
vectorstore = Chroma.from_documents(sentence_docs, embeddings)
retriever = vectorstore.as_retriever(search_kwargs={"k": 3})

def retrieve_with_window(query: str) -> list[Document]:
    """Retrieve and swap sentence text for window text."""
    results = retriever.invoke(query)
    for doc in results:
        doc.page_content = doc.metadata["window"]  # replace before LLM call
    return results
Choosing the Right Window Size

The window size k determines how many sentences on each side of the matched sentence are included in the context. A window of k=1 gives 3 sentences total; k=3 gives 7 sentences. The right value depends on the sentence density of your corpus and the LLM context budget. Use a window large enough that the relevant explanation is rarely split across the window boundary, but small enough that the context window is not dominated by off-topic surrounding text.
k (each side)
Total sentences
Approx tokens
Best for
FAQ-style short sentences
3 (default)
Technical documentation, papers
Dense narrative text, books
Sentence-Window vs. Parent-Child Chunking

Sentence-window and parent-child are related strategies but differ in how context boundaries are defined. Sentence-window uses a fixed sliding window centred on the matched sentence — the context is always the k nearest neighbours regardless of paragraph structure. Parent-child chunking uses explicit structural boundaries — the context is always the full parent chunk that contains the matched child. Sentence-window is simpler to implement and works well when document structure is irregular; parent-child is better when documents have natural logical sections that should not be split across contexts.

# Sentence-window: context = fixed k-sentence sliding window
# Good for: dense paragraphs, prose, continuous narrative
sentence_window_retriever = build_sentence_window_retriever(docs, window_size=3)

# Parent-child: context = full parent chunk from doc store
# Good for: structured docs, API references, step-by-step guides
from langchain.retrievers import ParentDocumentRetriever
parent_child_retriever = ParentDocumentRetriever(
    vectorstore=vectorstore,
    docstore=docstore,
    child_splitter=RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=100),
    parent_splitter=RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=512),
)

# A/B test both on your eval set to pick the right strategy
def compare_retrievers(query: str, relevant_ids: set[str]) -> dict:
    sw = recall_at_k(query, relevant_ids, sentence_window_retriever)
    pc = recall_at_k(query, relevant_ids, parent_child_retriever)
    return {"sentence_window": sw, "parent_child": pc}
Deduplication When Windows Overlap

When multiple adjacent sentences are retrieved for a single query, their windows may overlap significantly — the window of S3 and the window of S4 (both with k=3) share sentences S1–S6. Passing both windows to the LLM wastes context tokens on repeated text. Deduplicate by tracking sentence indices: if two retrieved windows overlap by more than 50%, keep only the one with the higher similarity score.

def deduplicate_windows(
    results: list[Document],
    overlap_threshold: float = 0.5,
) -> list[Document]:
    """Remove windows whose sentence ranges overlap too heavily."""
    kept = []
    for doc in results:
        idx = doc.metadata["sentence_idx"]
        k = doc.metadata.get("window_size", 3)
        new_range = set(range(max(0, idx - k), idx + k + 1))

        # Check if this window overlaps too much with any kept window
        redundant = any(
            len(new_range & set(range(
                max(0, kept_doc.metadata["sentence_idx"] - k),
                kept_doc.metadata["sentence_idx"] + k + 1
            ))) / len(new_range) > overlap_threshold
            for kept_doc in kept
        )
        if not redundant:
            kept.append(doc)
    return kept

# Usage
raw_results = retriever.invoke(query)
deduped = deduplicate_windows(raw_results, overlap_threshold=0.5)
for doc in deduped:
    doc.page_content = doc.metadata["window"]
Integrating with a Full RAG Chain

The sentence-window pattern plugs naturally into a standard LangChain RAG chain. The key is the window-swap step between retrieval and generation: retrieved documents have their page_content replaced with the window text before being formatted into the LLM prompt. Everything else — the prompt template, the LLM call, the output parser — works identically to a flat-chunk RAG chain.
Evaluating Window Size with RAGAS

The optimal window size is corpus-dependent. Too small and the LLM lacks the surrounding context it needs; too large and the window drowns relevant sentences in noise, degrading faithfulness. Run a grid search over window sizes (k=1, 2, 3, 5) on a labelled eval set, measuring RAGAS faithfulness and answer relevancy at each size. Faithfulness typically peaks around k=3 for technical documentation and drops as k grows past the point where the window begins including off-topic sentences.

from ragas import evaluate
from ragas.metrics import faithfulness, answer_relevancy
from datasets import Dataset

def eval_window_size(docs, eval_questions, ground_truths, k: int) -> dict:
    """Evaluate RAG pipeline at a given window size k."""
    retriever = build_sentence_window_retriever(docs, window_size=k)
    chain = build_rag_chain(retriever)

    answers, contexts = [], []
    for q in eval_questions:
        raw = retriever.invoke(q)
        deduped = deduplicate_windows(raw)
        ctx = [doc.metadata["window"] for doc in deduped]
        ans = chain.invoke(q)
        answers.append(ans)
        contexts.append(ctx)

    dataset = Dataset.from_dict({
        "question": eval_questions,
        "answer": answers,
        "contexts": contexts,
        "ground_truth": ground_truths,
    })
    return evaluate(dataset, metrics=[faithfulness, answer_relevancy])

# Grid search
for k in [1, 2, 3, 5]:
    scores = eval_window_size(docs, questions, truths, k)
    print(f"k={k}: faithfulness={scores['faithfulness']:.3f}  "
          f"answer_relevancy={scores['answer_relevancy']:.3f}")
Combining Sentence-Window with Re-Ranking

Sentence-window retrieval pairs well with cross-encoder re-ranking. Retrieve a larger candidate set of sentences (top-k=10), re-rank by relevance using a cross-encoder on the single-sentence text (not the window — shorter text is more diagnostic), then swap matched sentences for their windows and take the top-n after re-ranking. This gives you precision from the cross-encoder and context richness from the window.

from sentence_transformers import CrossEncoder

cross_encoder = CrossEncoder("cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2")

def sentence_window_with_rerank(
    query: str,
    retriever,       # sentence-level retriever (k=10)
    top_n: int = 3,
) -> list[str]:
    # 1. Retrieve sentence candidates
    candidates = retriever.invoke(query)  # page_content = single sentence

    # 2. Re-rank on sentence text (precise signal)
    pairs = [(query, doc.page_content) for doc in candidates]
    scores = cross_encoder.predict(pairs)

    ranked = sorted(zip(scores, candidates), key=lambda x: x[0], reverse=True)
    top_docs = [doc for _, doc in ranked[:top_n]]

    # 3. Swap sentences for their windows (rich context)
    windows = [doc.metadata["window"] for doc in top_docs]
    return windows

contexts = sentence_window_with_rerank("positional encoding in transformers", retriever)
Summary
Index single sentences for embedding precision; return the surrounding window for LLM context richness.
LlamaIndex: use
SentenceWindowNodeParser
MetadataReplacementPostProcessor
LangChain: build sentence nodes manually with window stored in metadata; swap before LLM call.
Window size k=3 (7 sentences) is a solid default for technical documentation.
Deduplicate overlapping windows when multiple adjacent sentences match a query.
